era_context: The mid-17th century continues with the consolidation of absolute monarchy in France under Louis XIV (personal rule began 1661). The Qing Dynasty is consolidating control over China following the Ming collapse. The Mughal Empire under Aurangzeb is expanding in the Deccan. The Ottoman Empire remains a major power in the Eastern Mediterranean and Eastern Europe. England is in the early reign of Charles II (Restoration, 1660–1685). Japan is in the peaceful Edo period under the Tokugawa shogunate.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Ottoman Empire activities in 1662 are less well-documented in Western sources
Sub-Saharan African events are poorly documented in European archives
Indigenous American events are poorly documented in European sources

events.title: Death of Cardinal Mazarin and Louis XIV's Personal Rule Begins
events.description: Cardinal Jules Mazarin, the chief minister of France and regent during Louis XIV's minority, died on 9 March 1662. Mazarin's death marked a crucial turning point in French history: Louis XIV, now 23 years old, took personal control of the government and began his personal rule, which would last until his death in 1715. Louis XIV's assumption of personal power in 1662 marked the beginning of the era of French hegemony in Europe. Louis immediately began to consolidate his authority, removing rivals and establishing the foundations for absolute monarchy. The year 1662 is thus a watershed moment in European history, marking the transition from the regency period to the era of Louis XIV's personal rule.
key_figures: Anne of Austria (Queen Mother, widow of Louis XIII)

sources.name: French royal records and official documents
Historiographic consensus on Louis XIV and the beginning of his personal rule

events.certainty_note: Mazarin's death on 9 March 1662 is well-documented in French records and contemporary sources. The date is certain.
cross_references: Precedes Louis XIV's expansionist foreign policy (1660s–1680s)
Related to construction of Palace of Versailles (1661–1710)

events.title: Kangxi Emperor Ascends Qing Throne
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor (r. 1661–1722) ascended the Qing throne on 5 February 1661 at age 7, following the death of his father, the Shunzhi Emperor. By 1662, Kangxi was consolidating his position as the legitimate Qing ruler and beginning to assert authority over the regency council. The year 1662 marks a crucial moment in Kangxi's early reign, as he was establishing the foundations for his later success as one of China's greatest emperors. Kangxi would eventually suppress the Three Feudatories rebellion (1673–1681) and expand Qing territory, making him one of the most successful rulers in Chinese history.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and the Kangxi Emperor's own writings

events.certainty_note: Kangxi's ascension to the throne on 5 February 1661 is well-documented in Qing records. The date is certain, though the year 1662 marks his first full year of reign.
cross_references: Related to Qing consolidation of China (1644–1683)

events.title: Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga) Dies; Ming Loyalist Resistance Weakens
events.description: Zheng Chenggong (1624–1662), known to Westerners as Koxinga, died on 23 June 1662. Zheng was a Ming loyalist who had resisted Qing rule and maintained an independent base in Taiwan and coastal China. His death marked a significant turning point in the Qing consolidation of China: with Zheng's death, Ming loyalist resistance was greatly weakened, and the Qing Dynasty was able to consolidate its control over China more effectively. Zheng's son, Zheng Jing, inherited his father's position but lacked his father's military genius and political acumen. The year 1662 thus marks a crucial moment in the Qing consolidation of China, as the last major Ming loyalist threat was eliminated.
key_figures: Kangxi Emperor (Qing ruler, beneficiary of Zheng's death)

sources.name: Qing imperial records and administrative documents
Ming loyalist records and chronicles (recorded later)
European merchant accounts (Dutch, Portuguese factors in East Asia)

events.certainty_note: Zheng Chenggong's death on 23 June 1662 is well-documented in Qing records and contemporary sources. The date is certain.
cross_references: Related to Qing consolidation of China (1644–1683)

events.title: Aurangzeb's Deccan Campaigns Intensify
events.description: The Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb (r. 1658–1707) was actively expanding Mughal control in the Deccan in 1662. Aurangzeb had established a major military presence in the region and was beginning to challenge the independent sultanates of Bijapur and Golconda. In 1662, Aurangzeb was also dealing with the rising power of Shivaji and the Maratha confederacy. The year 1662 marks a crucial phase in Aurangzeb's long campaign to bring the Deccan under Mughal control, a process that would continue for decades and eventually lead to the Mughal-Maratha Wars (1680s–1707).
key_figures: Shivaji (Maratha leader, emerging as major opponent)
Ali Adil Shah II (Sultan of Bijapur)

sources.name: Mughal court records and administrative documents
Marathi chronicles and oral traditions (recorded later)
European merchant accounts (Portuguese, English, Dutch factors in India)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns is well-established, but precise dating of individual campaigns and engagements in 1662 varies between sources.

events.title: Charles II of England Marries Catherine of Braganza
events.description: King Charles II of England married Catherine of Braganza, a Portuguese princess, on 21 May 1662. The marriage was a diplomatic alliance between England and Portugal, and Catherine brought a substantial dowry that helped stabilize English finances. The marriage was politically significant because it aligned England with Portugal against Spain and the Dutch. However, the marriage was also controversial because Catherine was a Catholic, which raised concerns among Protestant English subjects about the king's religious sympathies. The marriage produced no children, which would eventually lead to the succession crisis of the 1680s (the Exclusion Crisis).
key_figures: Catherine of Braganza (Portuguese princess, new Queen of England)

sources.name: English royal records and official documents
Portuguese court records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Charles II's marriage to Catherine of Braganza on 21 May 1662 is well-documented in English and Portuguese records. The date is certain.

events.title: Royal Society of London Receives Royal Charter
events.description: The Royal Society of London received its Royal Charter from King Charles II on 15 July 1662, formally establishing it as the premier scientific institution in England. The Royal Society had been founded informally in 1660, but the Royal Charter of 1662 gave it official status and royal patronage. The Society's charter established its mission to advance natural knowledge through experimentation and observation. The Royal Society would become the leading scientific institution in Europe and would play a crucial role in the scientific revolution. The year 1662 marks a crucial moment in the institutionalization of science in England.
key_figures: Charles II (King of England, granted charter)
Robert Hooke (natural philosopher, curator of experiments)

sources.name: Royal Charter of the Royal Society (15 July 1662)
Royal Society records and meeting minutes
Historiographic consensus on the Royal Society and scientific revolution

events.certainty_note: The Royal Society's receipt of its Royal Charter on 15 July 1662 is well-documented in official records. The date is certain.
cross_references: Related to founding of Royal Society (1660)

disconfirming_evidence: The death of Cardinal Mazarin is sometimes portrayed as a sudden and complete transfer of power to Louis XIV, but modern scholarship emphasizes that Mazarin's influence continued even after his death through his protégés and allies. Louis XIV's assumption of personal power in 1662 was not as sudden or complete as traditional narratives suggest. Kangxi's early reign is sometimes portrayed as a period of weakness and regency, but modern scholarship emphasizes that Kangxi was actively consolidating power from the beginning. Zheng Chenggong's death is sometimes portrayed as the end of Ming loyalist resistance, but Ming loyalist resistance continued in various forms until the Qing conquest of Taiwan in 1683. Charles II's marriage to Catherine of Braganza is sometimes portrayed as a purely political alliance, but modern scholarship emphasizes the personal and emotional dimensions of the marriage.
historiographic_note: The year 1662 is moderately well-documented in Western European sources, particularly for France and England. The death of Cardinal Mazarin and the Royal Society's receipt of its Royal Charter are well-documented in French and English records. However, sources for Asia (Mughal India, Qing China, Tokugawa Japan) are less abundant in Western archives and rely more heavily on indigenous chronicles and administrative records. Ottoman sources for this period are less frequently consulted in Western historiography, representing a potential gap in coverage. Sub-Saharan African events are poorly documented in European sources, representing a significant gap in coverage.

graph_edges.note: Louis XIV's assumption of personal power in 1662 led to his expansionist foreign policy, including the War of Spanish Succession (1667–1668)
Kangxi's consolidation of power in 1662 enabled him to suppress the Three Feudatories Rebellion (1673–1681)
Zheng Chenggong's death in 1662 weakened Ming loyalist resistance, leading to Qing conquest of Taiwan (1683)
Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns of 1662 escalated into the Mughal-Maratha Wars (1680s–1707)
Charles II's marriage to Catherine of Braganza in 1662 (which produced no children) contributed to the Exclusion Crisis (1679–1681)
Royal Society's receipt of Royal Charter (1662) led to publication of Philosophical Transactions (1665)
